By Shawn Twing
Before we dive into the content, let’s start with the big picture.
This is the 30,000′ view of the foundational principles that
translate SOI as an idea into SOI as an actionable way to exchange
value with an audience using paid traf�c.
SOI is the method. Paid traf�c is the mechanism.
Why Paid vs. Organic Traf�c?
I’m glad you asked.
All traf�c is paid for with some kind of currency — usually time or
dollars. That means the question isn’t paid vs. free. The question is
how am I going to pay for my traf�c — with more time than
dollars, or more dollars than time?
You’ve already invested time to create a high-value Multi Page
Presell Site (MPPS) for your business. I’m going to show you how
to invest dollars to speed up your results and get many other
bene�ts along the way.
André has covered organic traf�c. Paid vs. organic isn’t either/or,
The Big Picture
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 21 --

it’s both/and.
Within the eight lessons of this program I will be teaching in two
different ways.
One approach will focus on what I think. That’s the collection of
themes, principles, ideas, mental models, and strategies I have
developed while spending two decades and 40,000+ hours in my
�eld.
The second approach is an emphasis on how I think.
That’s not always as clear, and often will require that I “think out
loud” so you can understand how I reached a conclusion or
developed an idea. That will require a more conversational, less
polished, writing style. If it seems like I’m wandering around
occasionally, that’s me inviting you into my inner-world.
A word of warning — it’s not always pretty, but I do believe you’ll
�nd value in there among the occasional crazy.
I don’t like talking about myself — at all — so I’ll preface the
following comments with a quote from Tony Robbins, who often
says he’s not trying to impress anyone with his
accomplishments, he’s simply trying to “impress upon them”
what he has learned.
Here are a few things about me you should know in order to put
everything that follows in context.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 21 --

I have owned a digital marketing agency since 1998, managed
many tens of millions of dollars in client ad spend across every
major advertising platform. And I have created, optimized, and
scaled traf�c campaigns in dozens of different B2B and B2C
markets for clients large and small in some of the most
demanding, competitive markets imaginable.
My experience is broad across different business models,
marketing strategies, and business offers, and I’ve worked with
some of the biggest names in digital marketing.
The examples I share in this training are based on real campaigns,
with real clients who produced real results (good and bad). I can’t
share speci�c client information for obvious reasons, but each
example you’ll read about is 100% real.
More importantly, the ideas and examples I’ll share in this
training are not one-off unicorns that are dif�cult or even
impossible to repeat.
(We all get lucky occasionally, but luck isn’t a reliable, repeatable
strategy.)
Instead, I have shared representative examples that convey
deeper, underlying principles that produce real, consistent results
over and over again. You’ll learn about my epic failures as well as
my successes because both contain powerful lessons you can
transform into real, actionable insights for your business.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 21 --

(One caveat — when I say I, me, my, etc., I am referring to my team as well.
I am privileged to work with an amazing team of people and many of the
examples I’ll share were inspired and executed by my employees, not by
me.)
What This Training ISN’T
OK, with that out of the way, let’s talk about what this training
isn’t.
It is not Google or Facebook “how to” training. I am assuming you
or someone you work with knows how to do the mechanical work
to setup advertising campaigns the way you’ll learn in these
lessons. You can �nd or hire that elsewhere.
What This Training IS
This training is focused on teaching you a way to think about
and construct paid traf�c campaigns that perfectly leverages
the many unique advantages SOI creates.
Leverage — that’s a word thrown around a lot. Let me be very clear
what I mean when I use it here.
Leverage is the difference between incremental and
exponential. It’s the difference between a traf�c strategy that
produces 10% better results year over year vs. 10x results year
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 21 --

over year.
I don’t know your business and I can’t do your push ups for you
which means I can’t tell you what results you’ll get, but the
training in this section is designed to create improvements closer
to 10x than 10%.
Four Principles of Traf�c Generation
The next seven lessons in this section are based on four principles
I have identi�ed and re�ned during the last two decades. Please
take the time to understand and deeply internalize these
principles. I promise they can transform your business, and they
will set the stage for everything that follows.
Principle #1: If You Can’t Convert Your Perfect Pop,
It’s Your O�er That Needs Tuning
This is my personal favorite.
Please write this down and put it somewhere where you’ll
see it every day. If there’s only one thing you learn from me in
this entire training, I hope it’s this:
If you can’t convert the perfect audience, don’t waste your time,
energy, and dollars trying to convert an imperfect audience.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 21 --

Let’s use an example.
Assume you own a business that sells red widgets online, and
because widgets are light and margins are great, you also offer
free shipping.
You then setup a Google AdWords account and target the best
possible audience — people who are searching for “buy red
widgets online free shipping”.
And, just to be safe you decide to geo-target that audience so that
anyone who sees your Google ad is physically close enough to
your warehouse to receive their order in one day.
Now, imagine this scenario — a prospect searches Google for “buy
red widgets online free shipping”, they see your ad and it
con�rms that you, in fact, sell red widgets online, you offer free
shipping, and your ad even says “free NEXT DAY shipping”
because you know they’re only seeing the ad because they’re close
enough to receive your shipments the very next day.
What happens next?
One-hundred clicks later, not a single order has been placed.
What do you do?
If you’re like a lot of people, you decide AdWords doesn’t work.
Maybe Facebook would be better. Or maybe it’s the Google
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 21 --

Display Network, or LinkedIn.
Didn’t you read an article last week about a company that 10x’d
their results using Twitter? (It was a food truck, but who cares,
Twitter could work…) Or that last conference you went to when a
speaker mentioned everyone should use “retargeting”. Yeah, that’s
it — I should try retargeting instead. Stupid AdWords … why did I
waste my time with that?!
I wish I were joking, but I have heard variations of this
conversation more times than I can possibly count.
Here is the idea I would like you to commit to from this moment
forward — if you can’t convert the perfect audience for your
offer, the problem isn’t your audience, the PROBLEM IS YOUR
OFFER.
When I say offer, that doesn’t necessarily mean what you’re
selling eventually.
An offer is an exchange of value which begins with the
prospect’s attention. If the traf�c you’re buying doesn’t offer you
their attention by consuming all of your SOI content, your offer
isn’t a match with their needs and desires. If prospects consume
your content but don’t opt in, the opt in offer isn’t a match (or it
isn’t compelling enough to exchange the next level of attention).
The whole point of buying traf�c is to purchase the attention of
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 21 --

an audience. If you’re not getting that attention then look at the
offer you’re making �rst before moving on to other sources of
traf�c.
Remember this: traf�c solutions don’t solve offer problems.
Principle #2: Hyper-Segment Your Audience
This is an extension of the �rst principle, but it’s important
enough to stand alone.
In order to know how to grow your business, you have to hyper-
segment your audiences and track those segments
individually.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 21 --

The metaphor I use is a stock portfolio.
Imagine you have a portfolio with two stocks — 100 shares of
Company A and 100 shares of Company B.
However, you can’t see those individual stocks because they’re
combined into a single stock fund. At the beginning of the month
your portfolio was worth $2,000, and at the end of the month your
portfolio was worth $2,000. Net gain: $0.
What’s the �rst conclusion you might reach?
Investing isn’t pro�table.
[This reminds me of one of my favorite jokes about two people who load
up their truck with vegetables they buy for $2/lb. Then sell those vegetables
at a local farmers’ market for $2/lb. At the end of the day, after counting
their money and realizing their net pro�t was $0, one person looks at the
other and says “I think we need a bigger truck”.]
Now let’s look a little closer.
Company A stock did very well. Those shares increased in value
by 20%. Your 100 shares of company A were worth $1,000 at the
beginning of the month, and $1,200 at the end of the month.
Company B performed poorly. Those shares decreased in value by
20%. The 100 shares worth $1,000 at the beginning of the month
were worth $800 at the end of the month.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 21 --

Do you think you might reach a different conclusion about your
investments with this information?
How do we apply this to paid traf�c?
We hyper-segment our audience in order to determine how
that audience performs by segment, and then over time we
allocate ad spend based on historical return on investment.
The budgets for audiences that perform well are never diluted by
the budgets previously allocated to audiences that underperform.
The tool I use for audience segmentation is a simple grid.
You can use a digital tool like Excel or Google Sheets, or analog
(paper, white board, etc.). The grid includes the factors you believe
are most relevant in de�ning your audience — for search that’ll be
keywords organized thematically.
For Facebook, that’s often (at a minimum) gender, age, geography,
and interests.
For example:
▪ Segment 1: women, 35-40, U.S., East Coast, interested in yoga
▪ Segment 2: women, 35-40, U.S., West Coast, interested in yoga
Now imagine your business is online Yoga training and you live
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 21 --

in Los Angeles. Hyper-segmenting your audience will give you a
clue if geography matters (in my experience, it does, even when
there’s no reason other than geographic af�nity to explain why).
Thinking in segments is Criss Angel-level street magic (if you
de�ne your segments in ways that are veri�ably meaningful for
your business). Lots more about how to do that later.
Principle #3: Knowledge is Power
Knowledge is power, and there is a tremendous amount to be
learned from tra�c data when it is segmented correctly.
I might learn, for example, that a particular audience consumes
my content, opts-in, but then doesn’t buy — and another audience
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 21 --

consumes my content, opts in, and buys a lot.
Over time, these insights add up and I begin to develop a
“scaffolding” of knowledge about my business, my audience, and
my customers that I can then incorporate it into my strategy and
act on it.
Here’s a real-world example:
I worked with a national consumer goods product company that
had a very speci�c audience in mind for their product. They did
extensive (and expensive!) focus group work to con�rm that their
intended target audience was perfect.
Focus group participants uniformly loved everything about the
company and their product ideas.
When that company began to advertise online, that focus group-
informed audience was perfect in almost every way.
Using the “qualitative trifecta” (time on site, average pages
viewed, and bounce rate) I con�rmed that this audience stayed on
the site the longest, saw the most content, and had a single digit
bounce rate (meaning less than 10% left the client’s website
without seeing at least two pages).
The only thing that audience didn’t do was buy. Anything.
(Business lesson #1: you don’t have a business until you have a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 21 --

paying customer. Until then you have an expensive hobby.)
Think about that for a moment!
Tens of thousands of dollars and months spent identifying a
potential audience, interacting with and getting feedback from
audience members, building messaging and brand creative based
on that feedback, and developing an extensive web presence (FB,
website, online product store), only to �nd out that your intended
audience loved your ideas — but not enough to actually buy your
products.
Meanwhile, another audience among the dozens we tested for
that client didn’t look anything like their target market (different
gender, different age group, different geographic location, and
very different interests).
That audience didn’t spend as much time on the site and didn’t
consume as much content. From a content perspective, they were
not as engaged as the focus group audience — not even close.
However, one thing they did do was buy the company’s products
— in large quantities, and often.
That data was worth in�nitely more than the positive ROI
that traf�c campaign generated because it pointed the
company in a completely new, informed, and pro�table
direction.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 21 --

Because they segmented their audience, reported performance by
segment, and acted on the information they learned from the
wildly different performance, they were able to take an entirely
different approach to their marketing (which included
introducing new products, new packaging, and new, larger
quantity options).
Let me say it again (this won’t be the last time) — knowledge is
power. And, when you setup your traf�c campaigns the way
you’ll learn in these lessons, you’ll gain usable knowledge every
step of the way.
Principle #4: Manage Complexity
Eradicate complexity everywhere. (This is just as much of a life
principle as it is a paid tra�c principle, but I’ll stay in my lane and
focus only on the latter.)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 21 --

I want you to imagine an onion.
Not just any ‘ole onion — this particular onion represents your
paid traf�c strategy.
Now assume that’s a $10,000 onion, meaning every month you’re
spending $10,000 on paid traf�c across dozens and dozens of
campaigns.
After many months/years of doing this — some good, some bad
�nancially — you hire someone like me to evaluate what you or
the company you hired to create this “traf�c onion” have been
doing. You don’t want to stop doing it because something is
working, but you’re not sure what that something is.
Someone like me looks at this onion and the �rst thing we do is
deconstruct it, layer by layer, to see what’s really happening.
(Remember that scene in the movie Christmas Vacation when
Chevy Chase hands the Christmas lights to Russ … ?)
One layer/campaign is a high-performer, another is a disaster.
A few are mediocre, a few more are a little better while a few
others are a little worse.
Some layers make sense.
Others don’t. Layer by layer by layer I deconstruct what you’re
doing, all the while you’re spending $10,000/month on all of
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 21 --

the layers combined — good, bad, and ugly.
This example represents 90% or more of the client accounts I
am hired to evaluate.
Layer upon layer upon layer. Some ideas were ill-conceived from
the start and never performed well. Others were amazing for the
�rst 90 days, put on a pedestal, and have burned cash for the last
three years they’ve been on “auto-pilot.” You get the idea.
Here’s an example that’s sadly representative.
A colleague asked me to do a quick, 20-minute “drive-by” review of
one of his client’s AdWords accounts. That client had been
spending $36,000/year on AdWords for six years, breaking even
some years, underperforming in others, and only one year
showing positive ROI.
Fifteen minutes into the conversation I found a campaign that
had been responsible for a disproportionate amount of ad spend.
Two minutes later I showed the client six years’ worth of data
con�rming they had spent more than $50,000 advertising to an
audience looking for the homeowner version of the (much more
expensive) commercial product they sold.
Why?
Because the person who created the account didn’t know the
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 21 --

(subtle) difference expressed in the search queries, and everyone
who had managed the account since wasn’t able to see that
information easily. Twenty minutes later ad spend fell by
$11,800/year with 2-3 clicks of a mouse, and ROI was instantly
(and signi�cantly) positive.
Is that a one-o� unicorn?
No. It’s representative of more than half of the accounts I see, and
it’s usually much worse.
Layer by layer ideas are added individually and evaluated in
aggregate.
People forget.
Data isn’t immediately visible or understandable. Campaign
intent is forgotten. Strategies don’t evolve. Data gets muddy.
Waste creeps in, then it grows and grows and grows over time.
What’s the alternative?
Let’s imagine a different onion and a totally different approach.
Instead of taking underperforming layers o�, we’re only adding
layers on that have proven their value.
Layer #1 is whatever perfect is for your business.
It’s a single, standalone campaign that you know represents the
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 21 --

audience that should be perfect.
It’s the gold standard for your business, and you know you can
reliably spend whatever amount of money needs to be spent to
get all of that traf�c, because it’s the highest quality possible.
Businesses are built on the quality of this �rst layer of traf�c.
(Businesses also fail when they ignore it or take this tra�c for granted.)
Once that layer is setup, running well, and your reporting for it is
accurate, you then add on layer #2.
Layer #2 is whatever you think is 90% perfect.
In our red widgets example, layer #2 may be a search for “buy red
widgets online free shipping” in the surrounding area where your
company delivers in two days.
Strikingly similar to layer #1 (because you’re building on that
proven success and your deep understanding of why it’s
successful). Just different enough that you know performance
might be different (and you don’t want to dilute your reporting
accuracy).
Then another layer, another layer, another layer.
Each one clearly de�ned, 100% reportable so that you can see the
performance and value of every campaign you’re running
individually, objectively, and in a way that you completely
www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 21 --

understand.
You know these campaigns so well you can look at the data and
describe performance like you’re telling someone a story.
Build success one layer at a time.
Stop the poor performers when they’ve consumed the least
amount of ad spend possible, and then turn your time, energy, and
attention to opportunities for positive results.
When you do this day after day the weeks, months, and years take
care of themselves.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 21 --

Before We Move On…
Everything that follows is built on these four preceding principles.
I know you’re anxious to dive in so I’ll wrap up this lesson with a
story.
Several years ago I walked into a store and noticed someone
wearing a t-shirt that said “You Don’t Know Me”, and it had the
of�cial seal of the U.S. Federal Witness Protection Program on it.
As I laughed to myself, I noticed the person wearing it — my good
friend Tim who I hadn’t seen in years. “Hey, wait a minute, I DO
know you…”
If you and I haven’t met I might not recognize you in the same
situation, but I’m going to make a bold statement and say that,
like my buddy Tim, I believe I know you too.
Or, more accurately, I know what you want.
I know what you want, because it is the same thing we all want.
It’s what attracted you (and me) to Sphere of In�uence in the �rst
place, and it underlies most of what we’re doing as entrepreneurs,
business owners, and anyone responsible for the success of a
business venture.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 20 of 21 --

Each of us wants the ability to create stable, predictable,
scalable results in our business.
That’s as simple as it gets.
The key element we want is CONTROL — a dial to turn, a button
to push, SOMETHING we can DO that reliably moves our
business in the direction we want to produce the outcomes we
want to achieve.
One-off opportunities that may (or may not) be repeatable, fuzzy
strategies that produce as much waste as positive results, and tips
and tricks that are obsolete days after we hear about them, won’t
get us there.
Instead, we need the ability to build a TRAFFIC ENGINE for our
businesses. A day-in, day-out method for producing increasingly
better results.
That’s what you’re going to learn in the next seven lessons. You’ve
built or you’re building the SOI-inspired machine — I’m going to
teach you how to add the high-octane fuel to it.
Buckle up.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 21 of 21 --
